# Cost Forecast — 2026-06-28

**Subscription:** Azure subscription 1 (`ae5e40cd-96ef-48d8-950a-2e22cf8f991a`)
**Currency:** GBP
**Baseline window:** 2026-06-21 → 2026-06-27 (7 days; survivor run-rate, ex-Cosmos)
**Generated by:** /cost-forecast

> **Caveat — read first.** This is the **first post-cutover reconciliation** of the Cosmos → Postgres migration (completed 2026-06-27). The good news: Cosmos has billed **£0.00 for two clean days** (27–28 Jun) — the migration saving is real and confirmed in actuals. The open item: the new Postgres server (`psql-town-crier-shared`) now **appears** in Cost Management (rows for 26/27/28 Jun) but every day is still **£0.00**, including the two full days. That is **first-billing data lag** for a new resource type, not a real zero — the B1ms server is `Ready` and serving prod. So the single largest line in this forecast — Postgres at **£0.445/day** — remains **rate-card-derived, not measured**. Yesterday's report expected the first Postgres billing line on 28/29 Jun; it has **not yet landed**, so reconciliation slips to ~29/30 Jun. Re-run then.

## Headline

| Horizon                    | Bottom-up forecast | Azure forecast | Delta    | Confidence |
|----------------------------|-------------------:|---------------:|---------:|------------|
| Rest of month (28–30 Jun)  | £3.10              | £0.23          | +£2.87   | Medium     |
| Next 30 days               | £31.02             | £70.17         | −£39.15  | Medium     |
| Next quarter (90 days)     | £93.07             | £130.07        | −£37.00  | Low        |

**TL;DR:** Spend has settled at its **post-migration floor of ~£1.03/day ≈ £31.5/mo**. The Cosmos → Postgres cutover is fully reflected in actuals now — Cosmos is dead (£0 on 27–28 Jun, down from a *growing* £30+/mo line), replaced by a fixed ~£13.5/mo Burstable Postgres server. Azure's own forecast is wrong in **both** directions, exactly as last run: it **understates** the next three days (£0.23 vs £3.10 — anchored on the artificially-low partial actuals of 27–28 Jun plus Postgres reading £0) and **overstates** the next 30–90 days by ~£37–39 (still extrapolating dead-Cosmos history, plus a phantom £7.43/day plateau across 12–21 Jul). Nothing changed in `/infra` since yesterday, so this report is a confirmation, not a revision: numbers are within pennies of the 2026-06-27 run. The one unfinished piece of business is verifying the Postgres bill once the first actuals land.

## Why Azure's forecast is wrong

- **Cosmos (`cosmos-town-crier-shared`) — destroyed, now two clean £0 days, still in Azure's projection.** Cosmos was the dominant cost: **£30.34 in the last 30 days** (up from £24.45 the prior 30 — it was still *growing* right up to the cutover). Daily actuals: ~£1.5/day on 24–25 Jun → £0.61 on 26 Jun (cutover) → **£0.00 on both 27 and 28 Jun**. No `Microsoft.DocumentDB` resource remains in the subscription. Azure's linear model still drags ~£1/day of Cosmos forward — the bulk of its £37–39 over-statement on the 30/90-day horizons. Commit `26b1604a` (2026-06-27, "destroy shared Cosmos account").
- **Postgres (`psql-town-crier-shared`) — registered, but still billing £0 (data lag).** Provisioned 2026-06-26 (`9e4e159c`), cut over 2026-06-27 (`9bb09651`). The resource now shows up in Cost Management — but all three days (26/27/28 Jun) read **exactly £0.00**, including the two full days. A `Ready` B1ms server serving prod does not cost £0; this is first-billing latency for a new resource type (typically 48–72h, sometimes longer). Bottom-up prices it from the verified rate card at **£0.445/day**. This is why Azure *understates* the near term — but it is swamped on the longer horizons by the Cosmos drag and the July artifact.
- **The £7.43/day spike, 12–21 Jul (Azure forecast only).** Azure's model again invents a ~10-day plateau at £7.43/day (and a smaller ~£1.7/day bump across 12–25 Sep) — seasonality/confidence-band artifacts extrapolating June's Cosmos growth ramp and dual-run churn. No current resource can produce it. Strip it and Azure's curve roughly agrees with bottom-up. This single artifact accounts for essentially all of the 30-day over-statement.
- **Old .NET apps long gone.** No `ca-town-crier-api-prod` / `-dev` lines in the window — decommissioned mid-June when Go took over. Correctly excluded from the forward run-rate.

The last-30 total (£43.46) is **history, not run-rate** — it's dominated by dead Cosmos (£30.34) and the June Container Apps dual-run. The forward steady-state is the bottom-up sum below (~£31.5/mo), roughly a quarter of which is the now-zero Cosmos line.

## Bottom-up breakdown by resource

Survivor run-rates are the mean of 21–27 Jun (7 days), excluding Cosmos. Postgres is rate-card (B1ms £0.0142/hr × 730 + 32 GB × £0.0991/mo, both verified today against the Azure Retail Prices API, uksouth/GBP). 28 Jun is excluded as a partial day.

| Resource | Service | Daily run-rate | Rest-of-month (3d) | Notes |
|----------|---------|---------------:|-------------------:|-------|
| `psql-town-crier-shared` | PostgreSQL | £0.445 | £1.34 | **LOW CONFIDENCE** — rate-card, 0 days of actuals (still £0 in Cost Management). B1ms Burstable, 32 GB, no HA, no geo-backup, 7-day backup. Reconcile next run. |
| `ca-town-crier-api-go-prod` | Container Apps | £0.243 | £0.73 | Stable; minReplicas=1 (warm prod, deliberate). ~£7.4/mo. |
| `acrtowncriershared` | Container Registry | £0.124 | £0.37 | Basic, fixed daily charge. 87 MB / 10 GB used (0.8%) — no overage. |
| `ca-town-crier-api-go-dev` | Container Apps | £0.110 | £0.33 | minReplicas=0 (scales to zero). Bursty: request-driven activation + deploy churn (0.02–0.22/day). ~£3.3/mo. |
| `job-tc-poll-prod` | Container App Job | £0.073 | £0.22 | PlanIt polling worker. Essential. ~£2.2/mo. |
| `job-tc-poll-bootstrap-prod` | Container App Job | £0.011 | £0.03 | Seed/bootstrap poll. |
| `job-tc-digest-hourly-prod` | Container App Job | £0.006 | £0.02 | Hourly digest. |
| `job-tc-digest-hourly-dev` | Container App Job | £0.004 | £0.01 | Dev hourly digest. |
| `log-town-crier-shared` | Log Analytics | £0.004 | £0.01 | At 30-day min retention; near-zero ingest. |
| `job-tc-pg-purge-dev` / `-prod` | Container App Jobs | £0.004 | £0.01 | **NEW** — Postgres TTL purge jobs (from migration). First seen 28 Jun. Negligible. |
| `job-tc-subscription-sweep-dev`/`-prod` | Container App Jobs | £0.004 | £0.01 | Daily lapsed-tier downgrade sweep. |
| `appi-town-crier-shared` | App Insights | ~£0.004 | £0.01 | Folded into Azure Monitor line. |
| `acs-town-crier-uk` | Email (ACS) | ~£0.001 | £0.003 | Transactional email. |
| Other jobs / SB / Storage / SWA | — | ~£0.002 | £0.01 | dormant-cleanup, daily digest, Service Bus (Basic), blobs, static web apps — all ~£0. |
| **Total** | | **£1.034** | **£3.10** | Survivors £0.589/day + Postgres £0.445/day. |

## Recommendations

The honest headline is unchanged from the 2026-06-27 run: **there are no material new cost-reduction opportunities.** The migration already executed the big one (a *growing* £30+/mo Cosmos line replaced by a fixed ~£13.5/mo Postgres server), and the surviving footprint is already at its sensible floor:

- Postgres is the **cheapest Burstable tier** (B1ms), 32 GB, no HA, no geo-redundant backup. Azure offers **no Reserved Capacity for the Burstable tier** (only General Purpose / Memory Optimized), so there is no reservation discount to capture.
- Dev and prod **share one** Postgres server, one ACR, one managed environment — already consolidated.
- ACR is Basic at 0.8% of the 10 GB quota; Service Bus is Basic; Log Analytics / App Insights at the 30-day minimum. Nothing to trim.
- `ca-town-crier-api-go-prod` minReplicas=1 (~£5–6/mo of the £7.4/mo) is a **deliberate** warm-prod / cold-start UX choice the owner has already made (tc-9u1r / tc-e972). **Not recommended for reversal**; flagged only for completeness.

No new beads filed — filing sub-£2/mo or zero-opportunity items would only add noise. Hygiene actions taken instead:

- **The one open thread is verification, not saving:** the Postgres bill is still unobserved (£0 in actuals for 26–28 Jun). The bottom-up forecast rests entirely on the rate card for that line. **Re-run `/cost-forecast` once the first Postgres billing line lands (~29–30 Jun)** to confirm £0.445/day and close the loop. (Offer `/loop`, not `/schedule` — a cloud agent lacks the `az` credentials this check needs.)
- **Updated `tc-0qnj`** (P4, "Consider a Postgres/RG cost budget") with today's evidence. The subscription-level **£50/mo budget already exists**; steady-state ~£31.5/mo sits at ~63% of it — healthy headroom. A per-RG/Postgres budget remains a nice-to-have, not urgent.
- **Orphan `id-town-crier-cosmos-data` managed identity** — still present, leftover from the Cosmos decommission. **£0 cost** (managed identities are free), so no cost bead; belongs with the existing Cosmos-teardown tidy beads (`tc-vh10` / `tc-hpd2.15`).
- **`tc-fxau`** still owns dev revision sprawl — **£0 direct cost** (dev scales to zero), weekly Monday cleanup; left as-is.

## Methodology footnote

- **Data window:** 60-day actuals (2026-04-29 → 2026-06-28), daily, grouped by ResourceId and by ServiceName (Cost Management query API). Azure forecast: 90-day horizon (forecast API), anchored 2026-06-28. Postgres rates verified against the Azure Retail Prices API (GBP, uksouth): B1MS compute £0.01420/hr, storage £0.0991/GB-mo.
- **Survivor baseline:** mean of 21–27 Jun (7 days), excluding Cosmos. 28 Jun excluded as a partial day (Cost Management lag). New `pg-purge` jobs (first seen 28 Jun) added at +£0.004/day.
- **Key exclusions (one-offs / dead):** all Cosmos spend (destroyed, now £0); old .NET apps (already £0); June Container Apps dual-run + migration churn (the £9.28 last-30 figure is *not* the forward run-rate).
- **Known gaps:** (1) **Postgres has zero observed cost** — the £0.445/day line is rate-card, not measured; first billing line now overdue (expected 28/29 Jun, not yet landed), reconcile on next run. (2) Pulumi `stack export` not run this pass; the live `az resource list` (35 resources) was ground truth, cross-checked against the cutover commits — no untracked/drift resources of material cost found (only the £0 orphan Cosmos identity). (3) Quarter horizon assumes flat traffic; real user growth or PlanIt ingestion changes will move it.
- **Generation note:** the analysis was run on 2026-06-28 and completed just past UK midnight; all data (actuals through 28 Jun, forecast from 28 Jun) is anchored to the 28 Jun snapshot, so the report is dated 2026-06-28 for internal consistency.
